era_context: The Mediterranean world is in the midst of major conflicts that will reshape the political landscape. The Third Punic War (149–146 BCE) is in its second year, with Rome besieging Carthage. In Greece, tensions between Rome and the Achaean League are escalating toward open conflict. The Seleucid Empire is in decline, and Parthia is rising. In China, the Han Dynasty under Emperor Jing is stable and prosperous. This is a year of transition and conflict in the Mediterranean, but relative stability in East Asia.

events.title: Third Punic War: Roman siege of Carthage intensifies
events.description: In 148 BCE, the Third Punic War (149–146 BCE) was in its second year, with Roman forces under Scipio Aemilianus conducting an increasingly intense siege of Carthage. The siege had begun in 149 BCE and would continue until 146 BCE. In 148 BCE, the Romans were tightening their blockade and preparing for the final assault. The Carthaginians were attempting to hold out, but their situation was becoming increasingly desperate. This year represents the middle stage of the siege, with both sides preparing for the final confrontation.
events.certainty_note: The Third Punic War and the siege of Carthage are well-documented in contemporary sources. The year 148 BCE is securely dated as part of the siege period.
cross_references: Follows from -149-001 (outbreak of Third Punic War)

events.title: Achaean League tensions with Rome escalate
events.description: In 148 BCE, tensions between the Achaean League and Rome were escalating toward open conflict. The League, which had attempted to maintain Greek independence, was increasingly at odds with Roman demands for submission. The Achaean League was preparing for military resistance, though the outcome was already largely predetermined given Rome's military superiority. The escalation of tensions in 148 BCE would lead to the Battle of Leucopetra in 147 BCE and the destruction of Corinth in 146 BCE. This year represents the final stage of Greek political independence before Roman conquest.
events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions between the Achaean League and Rome in 148 BCE is documented in Polybius, though the specific events of this year are less detailed than those of 147 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman conquest of Greece

events.title: Han Dynasty under Emperor Jing: continued prosperity
events.description: In 148 BCE, the Han Dynasty under Emperor Jing (r. 157–141 BCE) was in a period of economic development and administrative consolidation. The empire had recovered from the Rebellion of the Seven States (154–151 BCE) and was experiencing a period of relative peace and prosperity. This era saw the development of Confucian bureaucratic institutions, the expansion of trade networks, and the strengthening of imperial authority. The reign of Emperor Jing is characterized by careful fiscal management and the maintenance of peace with the Xiongnu through the 'heqin' policy.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty chronology is well-established. Emperor Jing's reign dates are securely fixed.
cross_references: Represents contrast between Mediterranean conflict and East Asian stability

events.title: Seleucid Empire under Antiochus VII: consolidation of power
events.description: In 148 BCE, Antiochus VII Sidetes (r. 145–129 BCE) was consolidating his power and preparing military campaigns to restore Seleucid authority. The empire was attempting to recover from decades of decline and internal conflict. Antiochus VII was planning campaigns against the Parthians and the Hasmonean Jewish kingdom to reassert Seleucid control over lost territories. However, the empire's resources were limited, and the rise of Parthia as a major power constrained Seleucid options.
key_figures: Antiochus VII Sidetes (Seleucid King, r. 145–129 BCE)
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Antiochus VII's reign is established through numismatic and textual evidence, though specific events of 148 BCE are less well-documented.

events.title: Parthian Empire consolidation under Mithridates I
events.description: In 148 BCE, Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE) was in the later stages of his reign, having transformed the Parthian Empire into a major regional power. He had conquered Mesopotamia from the Seleucids and was consolidating control over the Iranian plateau. His military campaigns and administrative reforms established Parthia as the dominant power in the Near East. The rise of Parthia was directly contributing to Seleucid decline and would eventually lead to a fundamental shift in the balance of power in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East.
key_figures: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is less precisely dated than Greek or Chinese sources. Mithridates I's reign is estimated c. 171–138 BCE based on numismatic and later textual evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader shift in Near Eastern power dynamics

disconfirming_evidence: The year 148 BCE is often presented as a significant turning point in Mediterranean history, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the major events of this period (the Third Punic War and the Achaean League conflict) were already well underway by 148 BCE. The Third Punic War had begun in 149 BCE, and the tensions with the Achaean League had been building for years. The year 148 BCE itself was not particularly eventful compared to 147 BCE (Battle of Leucopetra) or 146 BCE (destruction of Corinth and Carthage). The significance of 148 BCE is largely retrospective, assigned because it falls within the period of these major conflicts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 148 BCE is moderate for the Mediterranean world, with sources available but less detailed than for 147 BCE or 146 BCE. The record is sparse for the Seleucid Empire and Parthia. Chinese sources are abundant and contemporary. The year 148 BCE illustrates the challenge of ancient historiography: the significance of a year is often determined by the major events that occur in surrounding years, and years without dramatic events are often overlooked or poorly documented. The perspectives of non-elite populations and non-Mediterranean civilizations are largely absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Carthage, ongoing in 148 BCE, continued into 147 BCE and beyond
Escalating tensions with the Achaean League in 148 BCE led to the Battle of Leucopetra in 147 BCE
Roman military operations in North Africa and Greece were occurring simultaneously
Han Dynasty stability contrasts with Mediterranean conflicts
